personality_voice.book_baseline: Bill is established across the books as cool, calm, and competent - someone who defies expectations (Head Boy but rock-concert clothes, earring, ponytail). He is warm and familial but not sentimental; he speaks directly and practically. He has dry humor ('Do us a favor, Perce, and shut up'), is unflappable under pressure (calmly reporting Mad-Eye's death, methodically warning Harry about goblins), and carries quiet authority without bossiness. He teases Percy gently, jokes with his mother about his hair, and engages intellectually in Order discussions about goblins and politics. He is also capable of emotional breaks - his voice breaks when recounting Mad-Eye's death.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core of Bill's calm, direct manner. His introduction scene ('True enough. Owe it all to a werewolf named Greyback. Hope to repay the favor one day') captures his dry, understated humor and composure about his disfigurement. His goblin warning scene is measured and authoritative. His announcement of Mad-Eye's death is blunt and controlled. These are consistent with the book characterization.
personality_voice.difference: The film loses almost all of Bill's texture: the teasing of Percy, the banter with his mother about his hair, the intellectual engagement in Order discussions, the warmth of his Hogwarts nostalgia visit with Harry in GoF, the chess-playing and casual family presence. The film Bill is reduced to three functional appearances. His voice is preserved in those moments but the range of his personality - the humor, the warmth, the intellectual curiosity - is almost entirely absent.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core voice is recognizable in the limited material, but the film removes enough of the personality range that the character is substantially simplified. The dry humor survives in one line; the warmth, the teasing, the intellectual engagement, and the emotional range (including the voice-break over Mad-Eye) are absent. This is a meaningful reduction, placing the score in the 14-17 range.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Bill serves multiple narrative functions across the books: family anchor and eldest-son benchmark for Ron's insecurity (PS/CoS), Order operative with specialist goblin knowledge (OotP), active fighter at the Battle of the Astronomy Tower who is savaged by Greyback (HBP), and in DH the host and protector of Shell Cottage who manages the family's safety, warns Harry about goblins, recovers Mad-Eye's body with Lupin, toasts Teddy Lupin's birth, and actively facilitates the Gringotts mission. In GoF he comes to support Harry at the Third Task and immediately volunteers to go to Arthur after Fudge's denial. He initiates, decides, and acts throughout.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film retains Bill's role as Shell Cottage host and his goblin warning. He announces Mad-Eye's death. He appears at the Battle of Hogwarts briefly. These are real narrative functions. However, his GoF role (supporting Harry, volunteering to go to Arthur) is entirely absent. His active role in recovering Mad-Eye's body is absent. His management of the family's evacuation to Muriel's is reduced to a single line of exposition. His role in the Battle of Astronomy Tower is absent (the battle itself is compressed).
narrative_role_agency.difference: The film retains some agency - the goblin warning is his initiative, the Mad-Eye announcement is his - but removes the GoF agency entirely, removes the body-recovery mission, and reduces his DH family-protection role to exposition. His role as the Order operative who volunteers immediately in a crisis (GoF hospital wing) is gone.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: Role is recognizable but clearly reduced. Several specific acts of agency are removed or transferred to exposition. The score falls in the 10-13 range.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Bill's motivations are consistent: loyalty to family, commitment to the Order, professional pride as a curse-breaker with genuine goblin expertise, and love for Fleur. He left Egypt to come home and work for the Order - a sacrifice he makes willingly. His goblin warning to Harry comes from genuine expertise and concern, not rule-following. He is motivated by doing what is right even when it costs him (giving up field work, housing fugitives at Shell Cottage at personal risk).
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film preserves the motivation behind the goblin warning - he explicitly grounds it in his professional experience ('I know goblins... I've worked for Gringotts ever since I left Hogwarts'). His motivation to protect Harry and the family is implied by his presence and actions at Shell Cottage. His Order commitment is referenced in the kitchen scene ('The Order uses it now as a safe house').
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The film does not show the sacrifice of leaving Egypt, does not show the internal tension of housing dangerous fugitives while trying to protect his new wife, and does not show the emotional complexity of his injury and its effect on his identity. These motivations are present in the books but absent from the film, leaving his motivations simplified to 'loyal family member who knows about goblins.'
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are partially present but flattened. The professional expertise motivation survives; the sacrifice and personal cost motivations do not. Score falls in the 8-10 range.

character_arc.book_baseline: Bill's arc across the series moves from absent legend (PS-PoA) to present family member (GoF) to active Order operative (OotP) to victim and survivor whose identity is tested by Greyback's attack (HBP) to protector and host in the final crisis (DH). The HBP arc is the most significant: he is savaged, his appearance permanently altered, and the question of whether Fleur will still want him is resolved by Fleur's fierce declaration. He emerges from this with his relationship confirmed and his character - unchanged in personality despite physical change - explicitly noted by the narrator ('in personality he seemed just the same as ever'). His DH arc is one of quiet, steady service.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film introduces Bill already scarred (DH Part 1), collapsing his entire HBP arc into a single introductory moment. The attack, the hospital vigil, Fleur's declaration, Mrs. Weasley's acceptance - all of this is absent. The film Bill has no arc; he is a static supporting presence. His DH role as Shell Cottage host is present but compressed.
character_arc.difference: The entire HBP arc - the most dramatically significant thing that happens to Bill in the series - is eliminated. The film skips from his absence to his post-attack state without showing the arc. This is a major structural loss.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is not merely compressed - it is eliminated. The film shows only the endpoint of the HBP arc (scarred face) without the journey. This is a significant penalty, placing the score in the 8-10 range rather than lower only because his DH role as steady protector is partially preserved.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Bill's key relationships: (1) Fleur - a relationship that begins as surprising (she notices him at the Triwizard Tournament), develops through HBP into an engagement that tests Mrs. Weasley's acceptance, and is confirmed by Fleur's fierce loyalty after the attack. (2) Mrs. Weasley - warm but with ongoing friction over his hair and earring, resolved into mutual respect. (3) Harry - Bill becomes a genuine ally and confidant in DH, the person Harry trusts enough to be honest with about the mission's limits. (4) The Weasley family broadly - he is the benchmark eldest son. (5) Goblins/Griphook - professional relationship that gives him unique insight.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The film preserves the Fleur relationship only superficially - she calls him beautiful, he takes her on a thestral. The Mrs. Weasley friction is entirely absent. The Harry relationship in DH is partially preserved (the goblin warning, the Shell Cottage hosting). The family benchmark role is absent.
key_relationships.difference: The Fleur-Bill relationship loses its entire dramatic arc (the HBP development, Fleur's declaration, Mrs. Weasley's acceptance). The Mrs. Weasley relationship loses all its texture. The Harry relationship retains some function but loses the warmth and trust built across GoF and OotP. The relationship with goblins is reduced to a single warning scene.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The most important relationship (Fleur) is present only as a fait accompli without the dynamic that defined it. The Mrs. Weasley relationship is absent. The Harry relationship is partially preserved. This is a substantial alteration of key relationship dynamics, placing the score at 5-6.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Bill's complexity comes from several sources: the subversion of expectations (Head Boy who looks like a rock musician), the intellectual engagement with goblin politics (OotP dinner discussion), the quiet authority that doesn't need to assert itself, the warmth toward Harry that develops across GoF and OotP, the physical transformation in HBP and its psychological implications (unchanged personality, new dietary preferences), the sacrifice of his career to serve the Order, the management of Shell Cottage as a refuge under pressure, and the private moment with Harry about the goblin warning that shows both his expertise and his genuine concern.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves the subverted-expectations element (the scarred face with the dry joke about repaying Greyback), the goblin expertise, and the Shell Cottage hosting. It loses the intellectual engagement, the career sacrifice, the HBP transformation arc, the warmth built across GoF/OotP, and the family texture.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The film Bill is recognizable but stripped of most of what makes him interesting: the contradiction between his appearance and his role, the intellectual depth, the emotional range, the relationship complexity. He functions as a competent supporting presence rather than a fully realized character.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: Major flattening has occurred. The character is reduced to two functions: Shell Cottage host and goblin expert. The complexity that made him a memorable secondary character - the subverted expectations, the intellectual engagement, the emotional range, the relationship dynamics - is largely absent. Score falls in the 5-7 range.

lost_or_transferred_material: Bill's entire HBP arc: the Battle of the Astronomy Tower, Greyback's attack, the hospital vigil, Fleur's declaration of loyalty, Mrs. Weasley's acceptance of Fleur

Bill's GoF role: coming to support Harry at the Third Task, the Hogwarts tour with Harry and Mrs. Weasley, volunteering immediately to go to Arthur after Fudge's denial ('I'll go to Dad. I'll go now.')
Bill's OotP role: active Order operative, intellectual engagement in goblin politics discussions at Grimmauld Place, the family dinner dynamics
The Mrs. Weasley/Bill hair-and-earring argument that establishes his personality and their relationship
Bill's voice breaking when recounting Mad-Eye's death (book: 'Bill's voice broke'; film: delivered flatly)
Bill and Lupin going to recover Mad-Eye's body together
Bill managing the family evacuation to Muriel's and explaining the Fidelius Charm protections in detail
Bill's private moment toasting Teddy Lupin's birth and his warmth toward Lupin
The 'Rita Skeeter called me a long-haired pillock' anecdote establishing his self-deprecating humor
Bill's role as the family benchmark that drives Ron's insecurity (PS/CoS/OotP)
Bill's career sacrifice - leaving Egypt to work for the Order
The physical detail of Bill's post-attack personality being unchanged except for preferring rare steaks, which the film references only as a joke in the introduction scene
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 if a core book function or act of agency is transferred or removed): Bill's entire HBP arc and his GoF agency are removed, not transferred - this enforces a ceiling consistent with the 55 total.
Cap 1 (score above 85 if films remove a major part of complexity, agency, or key relationships): applies; total is well below this threshold.
comparative.key_observations: The film preserves Bill's surface identity - the scarred face, the dry composure, the goblin expertise - but removes almost everything that gave him depth across the books. His most dramatically significant moment (the HBP attack and its aftermath, including Fleur's declaration) is entirely absent, collapsed into a single introductory joke. His GoF role as Harry's genuine supporter and Order volunteer is gone. His intellectual engagement with goblin politics, his family warmth, and the texture of his relationships with both Fleur and Mrs. Weasley are stripped away. What remains is a competent, recognizable supporting presence who serves two functions: Shell Cottage host and goblin-warning deliverer. The core voice survives in isolated moments, but the character is substantially flattened.
